speaker: PRIAM
text: After so many hours, lives, speeches spent,

speaker: PRIAM
text: Thus once again says Nestor from the Greeks:

speaker: PRIAM
text: “Deliver Helen, and all damage else—

speaker: PRIAM
text: As honor, loss of time, travel, expense,

speaker: PRIAM
text: Wounds, friends, and what else dear that is consumed

speaker: PRIAM
text: In hot digestion of this cormorant war—

speaker: PRIAM
text: Shall be struck off.”—Hector, what say you to ’t?

speaker: HECTOR
text: Though no man lesser fears the Greeks than I

speaker: HECTOR
text: As far as toucheth my particular,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Yet, dread Priam,

speaker: HECTOR
text: There is no lady of more softer bowels,

speaker: HECTOR
text: More spongy to suck in the sense of fear,

speaker: HECTOR
text: More ready to cry out “Who knows what follows?”

speaker: HECTOR
text: Than Hector is. The wound of peace is surety,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Surety secure; but modest doubt is called

speaker: HECTOR
text: The beacon of the wise, the tent that searches

speaker: HECTOR
text: To th’ bottom of the worst. Let Helen go.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Since the first sword was drawn about this question,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Every tithe soul, ’mongst many thousand dismes,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Hath been as dear as Helen; I mean, of ours.

speaker: HECTOR
text: If we have lost so many tenths of ours

speaker: HECTOR
text: To guard a thing not ours—nor worth to us,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Had it our name, the value of one ten—

speaker: HECTOR
text: What merit’s in that reason which denies

speaker: HECTOR
text: The yielding of her up?

speaker: TROILUS
text: Fie, fie, my brother,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Weigh you the worth and honor of a king

speaker: TROILUS
text: So great as our dread father’s in a scale

speaker: TROILUS
text: Of common ounces? Will you with counters sum

speaker: TROILUS
text: The past-proportion of his infinite,

speaker: TROILUS
text: And buckle in a waist most fathomless

speaker: TROILUS
text: With spans and inches so diminutive

speaker: TROILUS
text: As fears and reasons? Fie, for godly shame!

speaker: HELENUS
text: No marvel though you bite so sharp at reasons,

speaker: HELENUS
text: You are so empty of them. Should not our father

speaker: HELENUS
text: Bear the great sway of his affairs with reason,

speaker: HELENUS
text: Because your speech hath none that tell him so?

speaker: TROILUS
text: You are for dreams and slumbers, brother priest.

speaker: TROILUS
text: You fur your gloves with reason. Here are your reasons:

speaker: TROILUS
text: You know an enemy intends you harm;

speaker: TROILUS
text: You know a sword employed is perilous,

speaker: TROILUS
text: And reason flies the object of all harm.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Who marvels, then, when Helenus beholds

speaker: TROILUS
text: A Grecian and his sword, if he do set

speaker: TROILUS
text: The very wings of reason to his heels

speaker: TROILUS
text: And fly like chidden Mercury from Jove

speaker: TROILUS
text: Or like a star disorbed? Nay, if we talk of reason,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Let’s shut our gates and sleep. Manhood and honor

speaker: TROILUS
text: Should have hare hearts, would they but fat their thoughts

speaker: TROILUS
text: With this crammed reason. Reason and respect

speaker: TROILUS
text: Make livers pale and lustihood deject.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Brother, she is not worth what she doth cost

speaker: HECTOR
text: The keeping.

speaker: TROILUS
text: What’s aught but as ’tis valued?

speaker: HECTOR
text: But value dwells not in particular will;

speaker: HECTOR
text: It holds his estimate and dignity

speaker: HECTOR
text: As well wherein ’tis precious of itself

speaker: HECTOR
text: As in the prizer. ’Tis mad idolatry

speaker: HECTOR
text: To make the service greater than the god;

speaker: HECTOR
text: And the will dotes that is attributive

speaker: HECTOR
text: To what infectiously itself affects

speaker: HECTOR
text: Without some image of th’ affected merit.

speaker: TROILUS
text: I take today a wife, and my election

speaker: TROILUS
text: Is led on in the conduct of my will—

speaker: TROILUS
text: My will enkindled by mine eyes and ears,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Two traded pilots ’twixt the dangerous shores

speaker: TROILUS
text: Of will and judgment. How may I avoid,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Although my will distaste what it elected,

speaker: TROILUS
text: The wife I choose? There can be no evasion

speaker: TROILUS
text: To blench from this and to stand firm by honor.

speaker: TROILUS
text: We turn not back the silks upon the merchant

speaker: TROILUS
text: When we have soiled them, nor the remainder viands

speaker: TROILUS
text: We do not throw in unrespective sieve

speaker: TROILUS
text: Because we now are full. It was thought meet

speaker: TROILUS
text: Paris should do some vengeance on the Greeks.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Your breath with full consent bellied his sails;

speaker: TROILUS
text: The seas and winds, old wranglers, took a truce

speaker: TROILUS
text: And did him service. He touched the ports desired,

speaker: TROILUS
text: And for an old aunt whom the Greeks held captive,

speaker: TROILUS
text: He brought a Grecian queen, whose youth and freshness

speaker: TROILUS
text: Wrinkles Apollo’s and makes pale the morning.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Why keep we her? The Grecians keep our aunt.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Is she worth keeping? Why, she is a pearl

speaker: TROILUS
text: Whose price hath launched above a thousand ships

speaker: TROILUS
text: And turned crowned kings to merchants.

speaker: TROILUS
text: If you’ll avouch ’twas wisdom Paris went—

speaker: TROILUS
text: As you must needs, for you all cried “Go, go”—

speaker: TROILUS
text: If you’ll confess he brought home worthy prize—

speaker: TROILUS
text: As you must needs, for you all clapped your hands

speaker: TROILUS
text: And cried “Inestimable”—why do you now

speaker: TROILUS
text: The issue of your proper wisdoms rate

speaker: TROILUS
text: And do a deed that never Fortune did,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Beggar the estimation which you prized

speaker: TROILUS
text: Richer than sea and land? O, theft most base,

speaker: TROILUS
text: That we have stol’n what we do fear to keep!

speaker: TROILUS
text: But thieves unworthy of a thing so stol’n,

speaker: TROILUS
text: That in their country did them that disgrace

speaker: TROILUS
text: We fear to warrant in our native place.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Cry, Trojans, cry!

speaker: PRIAM
text: What noise? What shriek is this?

speaker: TROILUS
text: ’Tis our mad sister. I do know her voice.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Cry, Trojans!

speaker: HECTOR
text: It is Cassandra.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Cry, Trojans, cry! Lend me ten thousand eyes,

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: And I will fill them with prophetic tears.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Peace, sister, peace!

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Virgins and boys, mid-age and wrinkled elders,

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Soft infancy, that nothing canst but cry,

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Add to my clamors. Let us pay betimes

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: A moiety of that mass of moan to come.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Cry, Trojans, cry! Practice your eyes with tears.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Troy must not be, nor goodly Ilium stand.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Our firebrand brother Paris burns us all.

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Cry, Trojans, cry! A Helen and a woe!

speaker: CASSANDRA
text: Cry, cry! Troy burns, or else let Helen go.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Now, youthful Troilus, do not these high strains

speaker: HECTOR
text: Of divination in our sister work

speaker: HECTOR
text: Some touches of remorse? Or is your blood

speaker: HECTOR
text: So madly hot that no discourse of reason

speaker: HECTOR
text: Nor fear of bad success in a bad cause

speaker: HECTOR
text: Can qualify the same?

speaker: TROILUS
text: Why, brother Hector,

speaker: TROILUS
text: We may not think the justness of each act

speaker: TROILUS
text: Such and no other than event doth form it,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Nor once deject the courage of our minds

speaker: TROILUS
text: Because Cassandra’s mad. Her brainsick raptures

speaker: TROILUS
text: Cannot distaste the goodness of a quarrel

speaker: TROILUS
text: Which hath our several honors all engaged

speaker: TROILUS
text: To make it gracious. For my private part,

speaker: TROILUS
text: I am no more touched than all Priam’s sons;

speaker: TROILUS
text: And Jove forbid there should be done amongst us

speaker: TROILUS
text: Such things as might offend the weakest spleen

speaker: TROILUS
text: To fight for and maintain!

speaker: PARIS
text: Else might the world convince of levity

speaker: PARIS
text: As well my undertakings as your counsels.

speaker: PARIS
text: But I attest the gods, your full consent

speaker: PARIS
text: Gave wings to my propension and cut off

speaker: PARIS
text: All fears attending on so dire a project.

speaker: PARIS
text: For what, alas, can these my single arms?

speaker: PARIS
text: What propugnation is in one man’s valor

speaker: PARIS
text: To stand the push and enmity of those

speaker: PARIS
text: This quarrel would excite? Yet, I protest,

speaker: PARIS
text: Were I alone to pass the difficulties

speaker: PARIS
text: And had as ample power as I have will,

speaker: PARIS
text: Paris should ne’er retract what he hath done

speaker: PARIS
text: Nor faint in the pursuit.

speaker: PRIAM
text: Paris, you speak

speaker: PRIAM
text: Like one besotted on your sweet delights.

speaker: PRIAM
text: You have the honey still, but these the gall.

speaker: PRIAM
text: So to be valiant is no praise at all.

speaker: PARIS
text: Sir, I propose not merely to myself

speaker: PARIS
text: The pleasures such a beauty brings with it,

speaker: PARIS
text: But I would have the soil of her fair rape

speaker: PARIS
text: Wiped off in honorable keeping her.

speaker: PARIS
text: What treason were it to the ransacked queen,

speaker: PARIS
text: Disgrace to your great worths, and shame to me,

speaker: PARIS
text: Now to deliver her possession up

speaker: PARIS
text: On terms of base compulsion? Can it be

speaker: PARIS
text: That so degenerate a strain as this

speaker: PARIS
text: Should once set footing in your generous bosoms?

speaker: PARIS
text: There’s not the meanest spirit on our party

speaker: PARIS
text: Without a heart to dare or sword to draw

speaker: PARIS
text: When Helen is defended, nor none so noble

speaker: PARIS
text: Whose life were ill bestowed or death unfamed

speaker: PARIS
text: Where Helen is the subject. Then I say,

speaker: PARIS
text: Well may we fight for her whom, we know well,

speaker: PARIS
text: The world’s large spaces cannot parallel.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Paris and Troilus, you have both said well,

speaker: HECTOR
text: And on the cause and question now in hand

speaker: HECTOR
text: Have glozed—but superficially, not much

speaker: HECTOR
text: Unlike young men, whom Aristotle thought

speaker: HECTOR
text: Unfit to hear moral philosophy.

speaker: HECTOR
text: The reasons you allege do more conduce

speaker: HECTOR
text: To the hot passion of distempered blood

speaker: HECTOR
text: Than to make up a free determination

speaker: HECTOR
text: ’Twixt right and wrong, for pleasure and revenge

speaker: HECTOR
text: Have ears more deaf than adders to the voice

speaker: HECTOR
text: Of any true decision. Nature craves

speaker: HECTOR
text: All dues be rendered to their owners. Now,

speaker: HECTOR
text: What nearer debt in all humanity

speaker: HECTOR
text: Than wife is to the husband? If this law

speaker: HECTOR
text: Of nature be corrupted through affection,

speaker: HECTOR
text: And that great minds, of partial indulgence

speaker: HECTOR
text: To their benumbèd wills, resist the same,

speaker: HECTOR
text: There is a law in each well-ordered nation

speaker: HECTOR
text: To curb those raging appetites that are

speaker: HECTOR
text: Most disobedient and refractory.

speaker: HECTOR
text: If Helen, then, be wife to Sparta’s king,

speaker: HECTOR
text: As it is known she is, these moral laws

speaker: HECTOR
text: Of nature and of nations speak aloud

speaker: HECTOR
text: To have her back returned. Thus to persist

speaker: HECTOR
text: In doing wrong extenuates not wrong,

speaker: HECTOR
text: But makes it much more heavy. Hector’s opinion

speaker: HECTOR
text: Is this in way of truth; yet, ne’ertheless,

speaker: HECTOR
text: My sprightly brethren, I propend to you

speaker: HECTOR
text: In resolution to keep Helen still,

speaker: HECTOR
text: For ’tis a cause that hath no mean dependence

speaker: HECTOR
text: Upon our joint and several dignities.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Why, there you touched the life of our design!

speaker: TROILUS
text: Were it not glory that we more affected

speaker: TROILUS
text: Than the performance of our heaving spleens,

speaker: TROILUS
text: I would not wish a drop of Trojan blood

speaker: TROILUS
text: Spent more in her defense. But, worthy Hector,

speaker: TROILUS
text: She is a theme of honor and renown,

speaker: TROILUS
text: A spur to valiant and magnanimous deeds,

speaker: TROILUS
text: Whose present courage may beat down our foes,

speaker: TROILUS
text: And fame in time to come canonize us;

speaker: TROILUS
text: For I presume brave Hector would not lose

speaker: TROILUS
text: So rich advantage of a promised glory

speaker: TROILUS
text: As smiles upon the forehead of this action

speaker: TROILUS
text: For the wide world’s revenue.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I am yours,

speaker: HECTOR
text: You valiant offspring of great Priamus.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I have a roisting challenge sent amongst

speaker: HECTOR
text: The dull and factious nobles of the Greeks

speaker: HECTOR
text: Will strike amazement to their drowsy spirits.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I was advertised their great general slept,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Whilst emulation in the army crept.

speaker: HECTOR
text: This, I presume, will wake him.
